title: GUIDELINES FOR THE SENSORY EVALUATION OF FISH AND SHELLFISH IN LABORATORIES

sections.section: GUIDELINES FOR THE SENSORY EVALUATION OF FISH AND SHELLFISH IN LABORATORIES
sections.summary: Document title and code (CAC-GL 31-1999) only; not a substantive provision.

sections.summary: Heading only: 'I. SCOPE AND PURPOSE OF THE GUIDELINES' (no substantive text in this entry).

Table of contents entry for 'III. PROCEDURES FOR SENSORY EVALUATION' listing sampling, preparation, cooking, and assessment subsections (no substantive provisions in this entry).

Appendix II heading only: reference documents listed (non-substantive here).

sections.section: GUIDELINES FOR THE SENSORY EVALUATION OF FISH AND SHELLFISH IN LABORATORIES
sections.summary: Provides the scope and purpose: guidelines for laboratory sensory methods to determine defects (including procedures involving cooking) for fishery products to promote uniform application of standards and inspection procedures; includes some provisions for products not covered by Codex standards and defines 'fish' to include finfish, crustaceans, and molluscs.

Specifies evaluation-area requirements: no sample preparation except final trimming, organization and ventilation to minimize distracting stimuli and influence from other personnel, neutral-colored surroundings, and working surfaces illuminated by daylight or artificial daylight, with any specific standard conditions met.

When odour or gelatinous state cannot be decided in the thawed uncooked state, a ~200 g portion should be sectioned and cooked without delay to an internal temperature of 65 using baking, steaming, boil-in-bag, or microwave methods; the product must not be overcooked and exact times/conditions should be determined by prior experimentation.

sections.section: 2 General Standard for Quick Frozen Fish Fillets, Annex A 'Sensory and Physical Examination'
sections.summary: Footnote/citation referencing the General Standard for Quick Frozen Fish Fillets, Annex A 'Sensory and Physical Examination' (non-substantive here).

sections.section: 3.4 PROCEDURES FOR THE ASSESSMENT OF PRODUCTS
sections.summary: States that standards/specifications identify product features and acceptance/rejection or grading criteria and requires assessors to perform consistent, systematic sensory assessments relative to species and to record features referenced in standards as well as other relevant attributes.
Specifies that fresh fish are normally assessed by appearance and odour, with spoilage-related appearance changes used for grading and characteristics listed in Table 1 of Annex I.

Requires frozen fish to be examined in the frozen state with attention to wrappings, glazes, discolouration, dehydration and signs of thawing/refreezing, and states thawed samples should be examined as for unfrozen products where appropriate while noting that freezing/thawing can alter appearance and gill odours that may not indicate reduced product quality.

Specifies handling of cooked samples (hold in closed container, cool to tasting temperature, keep warm if not assessed immediately) and requires recording appearance, odour and any unusual features, encourages tasting because some compounds are detectable only by taste.

Comprehensive section on training assessors for objective sensory analysis of fish and fish products, covering assessor selection, sensory acuity screening, skill development, monitoring, test materials, sample preparation, controlled spoilage and use of commercial reference samples.
Heading only introducing Objective Sensory Training; substantive requirements and procedures are provided in the subsections.

Defines objective sensory testing for fish and fish products and states that sensory training comprises assessor selection for basic acuity and allergy considerations, development of analytical sensory skills through familiarization and practice, and ongoing monitoring of assessor performance and consistency.

Specifies candidate assessor criteria: ability to perceive odours (not anosmic), basic tastes (not ageusic), normal colour vision, reliable sensory reporting, capacity to learn descriptive terminology, and to relate stimuli to causes; the first five are testable and the last is developed in product-specific training, with repetition and randomized presentations recommended during testing.

sections.section: 4.1.2.1 Screening for Perception of Basic Tastes
sections.summary: Requires screening for basic taste perception—emphasizing discrimination of bitter and sour which are critical for detecting early decomposition—using matching-standards tests with published concentration ranges summarized in Table 1.
Advises odour-perception screening using representative samples chosen to reflect common and fish-product defect odours and points to two example test methods described in Annex II.
Prescribes screening for normal colour perception using standard ophthalmologic tests (e.g., Ishihara, Farnsworth‑Munsell 100‑Hue) administered exactly according to manufacturers' instructions.

sections.section: 4.1.2.4 Screening Test for the Assessment of Texture
sections.summary: Describes texture screening by touch for attributes such as firmness and springiness, and cites the Tilgner ranking test (samples of increasing firmness, assessed by forefinger pressure) using permanent or food-derived samples.

Presents a model training syllabus with recommended hands-on exercises and emphasizes preparing authentic, preferably naturally occurring, controlled-spoilage samples (with proper records and temperature control) and inclusion of commercial reference samples to demonstrate a range of defect and taint intensities.
